title: Network Utilities
description: Practice using essential network diagnostic utilities including nslookup, dig, netstat, arp, and route commands for DNS queries, connection monitoring, ARP cache management, and routing table inspection.
starterCode:
# NETWORK UTILITIES EXERCISE

## Part 1: nslookup

1. The nslookup command is used to query _______ servers for name resolution information.
2. nslookup can perform both forward lookups (name to _______) and reverse lookups (IP to name).
3. A forward DNS lookup resolves a _______ name (like example.com) to an IP address.
4. A reverse DNS lookup resolves an IP address back to a _______ name using PTR records.
5. In nslookup interactive mode, the "set type=_______" command queries for mail exchanger records.
6. The "set type=_______" command in nslookup queries for all available DNS record types.
7. nslookup can specify an alternate DNS server by typing "server _______" followed by the server's IP address.

## Part 2: dig

8. The dig command stands for Domain Information _______.
9. dig is the preferred DNS query tool on _______ and macOS systems.
10. The dig command provides more _______ output than nslookup, including TTL values and query statistics.
11. dig +short produces a _______ output showing only the answer without extra details.
12. The dig command "dig example.com _______" queries for name server records.
13. dig +trace follows the complete DNS _______ process from root servers to authoritative servers.
14. The ANSWER _______ in dig output contains the actual DNS records returned by the query.

## Part 3: netstat

15. The netstat command displays active network _______ and listening ports on a system.
16. netstat -a shows _______ connections and listening ports, including those not currently active.
17. netstat -n displays addresses and port numbers in _______ form instead of resolving names.
18. netstat -b on Windows shows which _______ or process is associated with each connection.
19. netstat -s displays protocol _______ including packets sent, received, and errors for each protocol.
20. The modern replacement for netstat on Linux systems is the _______ command.
21. A connection in the _______ state indicates a fully established active TCP connection.
22. A connection in TIME_______  state indicates the connection is closing and waiting for final packets.
23. Many connections in SYN_RECEIVED state could indicate a SYN _______ attack.

## Part 4: arp

24. The arp command manages the _______ Resolution Protocol cache on a local system.
25. ARP resolves _______ addresses (Layer 3) to MAC addresses (Layer 2).
26. The "arp -a" command displays the current ARP _______ showing all known IP-to-MAC mappings.
27. A _______ ARP entry is automatically learned through network communication.
28. A _______ ARP entry is manually configured and persists across reboots.
29. "arp -d" is used to _______ entries from the ARP cache.
30. ARP _______ is an attack where a malicious device sends fake ARP replies to redirect traffic.

## Part 5: route

31. The route command displays and modifies the IP _______ table on a system.
32. The "route _______" command on Windows displays the current routing table.
33. On Linux, the command "ip _______ show" displays the routing table.
34. The _______ route (0.0.0.0/0) is the path used when no more specific route matches the destination.
35. The route _______ command adds a new static route to the routing table.
36. The _______ column in the routing table shows which network interface will be used for each route.
37. A lower _______ value in the routing table indicates a preferred route when multiple paths exist.
solution:
# SOLUTION

## Part 1: nslookup

1. The nslookup command is used to query **DNS** servers for name resolution information.
2. nslookup can perform both forward lookups (name to **IP**) and reverse lookups (IP to name).
3. A forward DNS lookup resolves a **domain** name (like example.com) to an IP address.
4. A reverse DNS lookup resolves an IP address back to a **host** name using PTR records.
5. In nslookup interactive mode, the "set type=**MX**" command queries for mail exchanger records.
6. The "set type=**ANY**" command in nslookup queries for all available DNS record types.
7. nslookup can specify an alternate DNS server by typing "server **address**" followed by the server's IP address.

## Part 2: dig

8. The dig command stands for Domain Information **Groper**.
9. dig is the preferred DNS query tool on **Linux** and macOS systems.
10. The dig command provides more **detailed** output than nslookup, including TTL values and query statistics.
11. dig +short produces a **condensed** output showing only the answer without extra details.
12. The dig command "dig example.com **NS**" queries for name server records.
13. dig +trace follows the complete DNS **resolution** process from root servers to authoritative servers.
14. The ANSWER **section** in dig output contains the actual DNS records returned by the query.

## Part 3: netstat

15. The netstat command displays active network **connections** and listening ports on a system.
16. netstat -a shows **all** connections and listening ports, including those not currently active.
17. netstat -n displays addresses and port numbers in **numerical** form instead of resolving names.
18. netstat -b on Windows shows which **application** or process is associated with each connection.
19. netstat -s displays protocol **statistics** including packets sent, received, and errors for each protocol.
20. The modern replacement for netstat on Linux systems is the **ss** command.
21. A connection in the **ESTABLISHED** state indicates a fully established active TCP connection.
22. A connection in TIME_**WAIT** state indicates the connection is closing and waiting for final packets.
23. Many connections in SYN_RECEIVED state could indicate a SYN **flood** attack.

## Part 4: arp

24. The arp command manages the **Address** Resolution Protocol cache on a local system.
25. ARP resolves **IP** addresses (Layer 3) to MAC addresses (Layer 2).
26. The "arp -a" command displays the current ARP **cache** showing all known IP-to-MAC mappings.
27. A **dynamic** ARP entry is automatically learned through network communication.
28. A **static** ARP entry is manually configured and persists across reboots.
29. "arp -d" is used to **delete** entries from the ARP cache.
30. ARP **spoofing** is an attack where a malicious device sends fake ARP replies to redirect traffic.

## Part 5: route

31. The route command displays and modifies the IP **routing** table on a system.
32. The "route **print**" command on Windows displays the current routing table.
33. On Linux, the command "ip **route** show" displays the routing table.
34. The **default** route (0.0.0.0/0) is the path used when no more specific route matches the destination.
35. The route **add** command adds a new static route to the routing table.
36. The **interface** column in the routing table shows which network interface will be used for each route.
37. A lower **metric** value in the routing table indicates a preferred route when multiple paths exist.
hints: nslookup queries DNS servers; forward lookup = name to IP; reverse lookup = IP to name (PTR records).
In nslookup, 'set type=MX' queries mail exchangers; 'set type=ANY' queries all record types.
dig (Domain Information Groper) is preferred on Linux/macOS; +short gives condensed output; +trace follows resolution.
netstat shows active connections; -a = all, -n = numerical, -b = application/process, -s = statistics.
ss is the modern Linux replacement for netstat; ESTABLISHED = active connection; TIME_WAIT = closing.
Many SYN_RECEIVED connections indicate a possible SYN flood denial-of-service attack.
ARP resolves IP (Layer 3) to MAC (Layer 2); 'arp -a' shows cache; dynamic = learned, static = manual.
ARP spoofing sends fake replies to redirect traffic; 'arp -d' deletes cache entries.
route print (Windows) / ip route show (Linux) displays the routing table; default route = 0.0.0.0/0.
Lower metric = preferred route; 'route add' creates static routes; interface column shows the outgoing NIC.
